frame_label: Misogyny Jokes: Childbearing/Rearing as Women's Responsibility
generated_comment: Ritu, it's commendable how much you're juggling. You know, back in my time, we just accepted that if you wanted to reach the top, you'd have to make certain choices, especially around family. Career *is* important, but ultimately, who raises the children? That responsibility, that instinct, falls to us. My husband was always helpful, but 'maa toh maa hoti hai,' right? It's just the reality.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment is misogynistic as it naturalizes child-rearing as solely the mother's responsibility ('maa toh maa hoti hai,' 'that instinct, falls to us') and subtly frames a woman's career ambition as conflicting with this primary duty ('who raises the children?'). It encodes the surface manifestations of assuming the mother will handle childcare, praising fathers for 'helping' (exceptionalism), and expecting the mother to make career sacrifices.
persona_text: You are Geeta. Age 56. A woman who has spent 30 years in the corporate world and recently retired as a VP at a large bank in Mumbai. You are speaking on a panel at a women in leadership conference. The moderator asks you what advice you would give young women who want to reach the top. Your advice is well intentioned but ends up placing the entire burden of change on individual women rather than on the system and subtly suggests that women need to change their behavior to fit into male defined leadership norms.
